perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party: (a) shall take all reasonable steps (defined below) to keep all Confidential Information strictly confidential; (b) shall not disclose or reveal any Confidential Information to any person other than its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in the Evaluation or who otherwise need to know the Confidential Information for the purpose of the Evaluation; (c) shall not use Confidential Information for any purpose other than in connection with the Evaluation. and (d) shall not disclose to any person (other than those of its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in the Evaluation or who otherwise need to know for the purpose of the Evaluation) any information about the Evaluation, or the terms or conditions or any other facts relating thereto, including, without limitation, the fact that discussions are taking place with respect thereto or the status thereof, or the fact that Confidential Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or its Representatives. As used herein “reasonable steps” means those steps the Receiving Party takes to protect its own similar proprietary and confidential information, which shall not be less than a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party: (a) shall endeavor to take steps to keep all Confidential Information confidential; (b) shall try not to disclose or reveal any Confidential Information to any person other than its Representatives; (c) shall consider not using Confidential Information for any purpose other than in connection with the Evaluation. and (d) shall attempt to not disclose to any person any information about the Evaluation. As used herein “steps” means any action the Receiving Party takes to protect its own information.
perturbation.explanation: The use of 'endeavor to take steps', 'try not to disclose', 'consider not using', and 'attempt to not disclose' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. It replaces a defined standard of 'reasonable steps' with a vague notion of 'any action'.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By weakening the language to 'endeavor,' 'try,' 'consider,' and defining steps as 'any action' the contract fails to provide adequate protection, which contradicts the requirement of reasonable efforts under CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'reasonable steps' to protect confidential information, defined as a 'reasonable standard of care.' The changed text weakens this obligation by using terms like 'endeavor to take steps,' 'try not to disclose,' 'consider not using,' and 'attempt to not disclose,' replacing the defined 'reasonable steps' with a vague notion of 'any action'. This change contradicts the requirement of maintaining secrecy as supported by California Code, CIV 3426.1, which addresses the duty to maintain secrecy. The modified contract language undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality provision, suggesting a weaker obligation than the original agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Receiving Party shall (at the Receiving Party’s election) promptly return or destroy (provided that any such destruction shall be certified by a duly authorized Representative of the Receiving Party) all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party and all copies, reproductions, summaries, analyses or extracts thereof or based thereon (whether in hard-copy form or an intangible media, such as electronic mail or computer files) in the Receiving Party’s possession or in the possession of any Representative of the Receiving Party; provided, however: (i) that if a legal proceeding has been instituted to seek disclosure of the Confidential Information, such material shall not be destroyed until the proceeding is settled or a final judgment with respect thereto has been rendered; (ii) that the Receiving Party shall not, in connection with the foregoing obligations, be required to identify or delete Confidential Information held electronically in archive or back-up systems in accordance with general systems archiving or backup policies; and (iii) that the Receiving Party shall not be obligated to return or destroy Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to the extent the Receiving Party is required to retain a copy pursuant to applicable law, and further provided that the Receiving Party will not, and the Receiving Party will use reasonable measures to cause its employees not to, access such Confidential Information so archived or backed-up.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Receiving Party may (at the Receiving Party’s convenience) return or destroy Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party might provide proof of destruction. However: (i) if a legal proceeding exists, the material may or may not be destroyed; (ii) the Receiving Party may not be required to delete archived electronic data; and (iii) if law requires retention, the Receiving Party might not destroy the data.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall' with 'may' and 'might', and changing 'promptly' to 'at the Receiving Party’s convenience' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. The removal of the requirement for a duly authorized representative to certify destruction, and softening of exceptions, further reduces the enforceability of this clause.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 17 GDPR - Right to erasure ('right to be forgotten')
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 17 of the GDPR grants individuals the right to have their personal data erased under certain circumstances. By using the terms 'may', 'might', and softening the requirement to destroy the data, this modified clause contradicts the GDPR's requirement for a clear and enforceable process for data erasure when requested by the data subject.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w
perturbation.justification: The original contract language ('shall promptly return or destroy') creates a clear obligation. The changed text introduces ambiguity by using 'may' and 'might,' weakening the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. The scraped law snippet from Art. 17 GDPR states "the data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay". By replacing 'shall' with 'may' and 'might,' the modified text contradicts the GDPR's requirement for a clear and enforceable process for data erasure, creating a significant contradiction, falling under the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Information being furnished to SAP and the Company, each of SAP and the Company hereby agree that, until twelve (12) months after the date of termination of this Agreement, such party will not solicit for employment with such party or any of its subsidiaries, or employ, any of the current officers or employees of the other party with whom such party has had contact during the term of this Agreement and who became known by such party or who was identified to such party as part of the Evaluation under this Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, nothing herein shall restrict or preclude either party from (A) making generalized searches for employees (by use of advertisements in the media, the engagement of search firms or otherwise), (B) continuing its ordinary course hiring practices that are not targeted specifically at employees of the other party or (C) hiring an employee of the other party who first initiates an employment discussion with such party, so long as such party has not violated the restrictions on solicitation contained in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Information being furnished to SAP and the Company, each of SAP and the Company will consider not soliciting for employment with such party or any of its subsidiaries, or employ, any of the current officers or employees of the other party with whom such party has had contact during the term of this Agreement and who became known by such party. This consideration is not a restriction, and shall not preclude either party from (A) making generalized searches for employees, (B) continuing its hiring practices, or (C) hiring an employee of the other party who initiates an employment discussion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agree that...will not' to 'will consider not' significantly weakens the non-solicitation agreement, making it non-binding and unenforceable. The added clause stating the 'consideration is not a restriction' explicitly allows for solicitation, defeating the purpose of the original clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements, which may include restrictions on employee solicitation if they effectively restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While the original text attempted to create a limited non-solicitation agreement, the modified text essentially negates this agreement, contradicting the spirit of protecting employee mobility that Section 16600 embodies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original non-solicitation agreement to a mere 'consideration,' effectively negating it. This contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which, as stated in the provided snippet, reads broadly to void the application of any noncompete agreement or clause that doesn't satisfy an exception. The change from 'agree that...will not' to 'will consider not' removes the binding obligation, conflicting with the protection of employee mobility that the law aims to provide, as explained in the Law Explanation. The snippet states: 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void,' which supports the determination that weakening the non-solicitation agreement creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree the courts of the State of California shall be the exclusive venue for disputes arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties may prefer that disputes arising under this Agreement be resolved in the courts of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be' to 'may prefer', the mandatory nature of the exclusive venue clause is removed, and it becomes a suggestion rather than a binding agreement. This introduces ambiguity and makes the venue for dispute resolution uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1404 allows a district court to transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought based on convenience and the interest of justice. An agreement on venue is a factor in determining the appropriate venue. By making the venue clause permissive rather than mandatory, the agreement has less weight in a court's decision regarding a motion to transfer venue under 28 U.S.C. § 1404, potentially leading to litigation outside of the agreed-upon jurisdiction, thus contradicting the original intent of the clause to have disputes resolved in California.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., §§ 119 , 163 (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 58, 36 Stat. 1103 ; Sept. 8, 1916, ch. 475, § 5, 39 Stat. 851 ). Section co
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be' to 'may prefer' weakens the mandatory nature of the venue clause. 28 U.S.C. § 1404 allows a court to transfer a civil action based on convenience and the interest of justice. The original text provides a clear venue agreement. The changed text, as explained, makes the agreement less binding, which contradicts the original intent to have disputes resolved in California. Both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 include: "For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented." This indicates that the venue can be changed. However, changing the language to 'may prefer' introduces ambiguity. Because the original text had a clearly defined venue, and the edited text makes it optional, this introduces a legal ambiguity.
